text:
🔭 Ethics & meaning across science, business, and art

None of the three is "ethical" as a whole — each has a live debate about where values enter, not whether they do.

• Science: the "value-free ideal" is now a minority view — Douglas argues values are unavoidably "saturated" through research wherever uncertainty forces a judgment call (Fact/source).
• Business: Friedman's shareholder-only doctrine has been losing ground to Freeman's stakeholder theory for two decades (Practitioner consensus).
• Art: autonomism vs. moralism vs. the now-dominant "moderate moralism" (Carroll) — an ethical flaw can be an artistic flaw, but isn't automatically one (Fact/source).
• Meaning, across all three: Wolf (worthwhile-project engagement), Weber (science as passionate vocation despite disenchantment), Nietzsche (art as the sole aesthetic justification of existence) converge on quality-of-engagement over domain-inherent virtue.
